lens.included_activities: Private operating companies that run RV parks and campgrounds for external guests, including short-stay and seasonal campsite accommodation, reservations, guest service, site utilities, washrooms, laundry, grounds, recreation amenities, camp stores, and related add-ons.
lens.excluded_activities: Public or nonprofit campgrounds, bare-land and residential mobile-home-site lessors, passive property-holding entities, non-control financing vehicles, captive internal facilities, recreational facilities without accommodation, and recreational or vacation camps classified outside NAICS 721211.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Direct-to-consumer accommodation sold by night, week, month, or season, with repeat visits and ancillary fees for site selection, amenities, rentals, retail, and activities; some parks also have longer-duration annual or membership-like arrangements.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for the broader NAICS 721200 group, not 721211 alone, while Anthropic observes Claude use across occupations rather than within campgrounds. I mapped the wage-bearing occupation groups to campground workflows, assigned higher exposure to office, reservations, customer service, bookkeeping, marketing, and portions of management, assigned low exposure to grounds, housekeeping, maintenance, food service, safety, and recreation delivery, and reduced the result for reservation and pricing automation already available in campground software. Inclusion of recreational camps in 721200 likely adds hands-on recreation and instruction and therefore biases exposure downward for RV parks; vendor evidence can bias the deduction for already-automated work upward.
a.rationale: The BLS mix is dominated by physical work: building and grounds cleaning and maintenance are 16.08% of employment, installation and repair 16.14%, and personal care and service 13.78%. The main digitally exposed pool is office and administrative support at 19.34%, plus parts of management at 12.00%, business and financial operations at 3.74%, and sales at 4.18%. Within office work, desk clerks, customer service, reservations, bookkeeping, and general office clerks are prominent. Anthropic separately reports high observed AI exposure for customer-service tasks in automated API workflows. Existing campground systems already automate booking, grid allocation, pricing, communications, and add-ons, so only the current not-yet-automated portion is counted.
a.change_evidence: An exact-721211 establishment survey linking payroll hours by workflow to current PMS, chatbot, pricing, bookkeeping, and scheduling usage, supplemented by time-and-motion interviews at 20 to 30 independent parks, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The latest accessible BLS industry occupation table is May 2023 and is four-digit NAICS 721200, which also includes recreational camps.
BLS excludes self-employed workers, material in small owner-operated parks.
Task exposure is judgmentally mapped from occupation groups and is not a direct measurement of hours saved in campgrounds.
Vendor descriptions establish technical availability, not representative adoption or realized labor displacement.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures AI use for U.S. employer businesses overall and by broad sector or firm size, not the five-year implemented share of exposed campground labor. I used the 2026 all-business usage range, the under-20% use rate among firms with four or fewer employees, the earlier low adoption in Accommodation and Food Services, and directly available campground automation products as anchors for a five-year implementation judgment. Broad-economy adoption may overstate small campground readiness, while the older sector result and a narrow definition of AI may understate adoption of embedded AI and rules-based automation.
rho.rationale: Census reported overall business AI use of 17% to 20% from December 2025 through May 2026 and less than 20% among firms with four or fewer employees. Its earlier survey found only 1.2% current and 2.3% planned use in Accommodation and Food Services under a narrower production-focused question. Implementation can rise because campground-specific systems already automate reservations, pricing, grid allocation, guest messages, reporting, and add-ons, but fragmented small operators still face data setup, integrations, staff training, service-quality escalation, and seasonal operating constraints.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal Outdoor Hospitality Industry or Campspot survey reporting adoption, active feature usage, labor hours before and after deployment, override rates, and guest-service failures by independent park size would materially change this estimate.
caveats: Census evidence is not specific to NAICS 721211 and survey wording changed in late 2025.
Installed software does not prove sustained workflow use or headcount avoidance.
Small, seasonal, owner-operated parks may have limited process standardization and capital for integration.

e.rationale: The official definition is inherently operating-company oriented: establishments operate sites for campers and may provide washrooms, laundry, recreation, stores, and snack bars, while bare residential mobile-home-site lessors and recreational facilities without accommodation are explicitly elsewhere. Most private firms in the injected size band should therefore serve external guests and fit the lens. The deduction allows for mixed-use properties, passive real-estate ownership, non-control structures, public or nonprofit operations misaligned with the private-firm population, and firms whose normalized operating earnings do not transfer cleanly with the land-heavy asset.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census or commercial database for the 34 injected band firms, coded for active operations, ownership of real estate and operating company, public or nonprofit status, revenue mix, EBITDA normalization, and control availability, would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source enumerates lens eligibility among firms in the injected EBITDA band.
NAICS is assigned at establishment level while eligibility and the injected count are firm-level constructs.
Campground value is often concentrated in land, complicating separation of transferable operations from property ownership.

s5.rationale: A 2025 sector investment report describes fragmented private ownership, a shift toward mid-market transactions of $1 million to $10 million, active private-equity and mid-market buyers, and sellers increasingly using seller financing. A campground association succession article also describes family ownership, generational transfer, owner residence on-site, and significant land value. These facts support real control-transfer activity but also show that family succession, emotional ties, property complexity, financing costs, and land-heavy valuations can delay or prevent a qualifying third-party transfer. The range is therefore a bounded judgment rather than an observed transfer rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched dataset from CoStar, MSCI Real Assets, Leisure Investment Properties Group, BizBuySell, and county deeds that distinguishes operating-company control sales from land-only sales, family gifts, internal reorganizations, and closures for NAICS 721211 firms in the target band would materially change the probability.
caveats: Broker reports are marketing-oriented and do not provide a population denominator or completed-sale frequency.
Property transfers do not always transfer the operating company or constitute a qualifying control event.
Succession evidence is from a Wisconsin association and may not represent the national owner-age distribution.

q.rationale: Campground revenue is generally posted as site accommodation and ancillary charges rather than cost-plus labor billing, so an operator can initially retain labor savings. Campground software also demonstrates that operators can adjust prices by occupancy, demand, and lead time and sell site locks and add-ons, supporting pricing discretion. Retention is capped because online marketplaces and comparable ADR and occupancy data increase price transparency, contracts renew frequently, guests can choose public, dispersed, or competing private sites, and some savings will be reinvested in service, amenities, or lower prices. The range represents the discounted five-year retained share of implemented gross benefit, not demand or implementation success.
q.change_evidence: Park-level cohorts showing five years of realized labor savings, rate changes, renewal and repeat-booking behavior, OTA share, guest acquisition cost, occupancy, and competitive openings after automation would materially change retention.
caveats: Campspot's reported revenue effects are vendor claims and may reflect selection into advanced pricing tools.
Retention varies sharply by local scarcity, zoning, seasonality, public-campground competition, and guest segment.
Annual and seasonal arrangements may retain gains differently from transient nightly bookings.

d5.bridge: KOA measures U.S. and Canadian household camping participation and spending, while OIA measures U.S. vehicle-accessible and RV camping participants, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for paid private NAICS 721211 services. I used the resilience and mixed 2024-2025 direction as a demand anchor, with a base near flat rather than extrapolating nominal spending. Participation can overstate paid private-park demand because it includes public and dispersed camping; the OIA RV decline can understate the mixed campsite basket because vehicle-accessible camping grew.
d5.rationale: KOA reported more than 11 million additional camping households in 2024 versus 2019, although camping was slightly down year over year; 65% planned to spend the same or more on travel. OIA then reported 42.3 million Americans camping close to vehicles in 2025, up more than 5% in a year and 6.2 million since 2020, while RV camping fell 6.4% year over year and returned to pre-pandemic levels. The current service basket includes both RV and tent sites, so the evidence supports broadly durable but not assured growth after removing price and quality changes.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. private-campground panel from Campspot, Newbook, RMS, or OHI with occupied site-nights by RV versus tent, constant-quality ADR deflators, openings and closures, repeat guests, and public-versus-private substitution would materially change the ratio.
caveats: KOA includes Canada and is commissioned by a major campground franchisor.
Participation and trip spending are not equivalent to paid private campground site-nights.
The five-year horizon is exposed to fuel and RV ownership costs, weather and wildfire, household travel budgets, and local supply constraints.

o.rationale: The service is tied to accountable operation of physical sites, utilities, washrooms, laundry, grounds, recreation facilities, retail, safety, maintenance, and guest issues. BLS shows large physical occupation shares in grounds and cleaning, maintenance and repair, recreation, food service, and protective service. Software and self-service can remove check-in and administrative touchpoints but cannot eliminate responsibility for the property, infrastructure, safety, cleanliness, rule enforcement, emergency response, and service recovery. A small share may migrate to dispersed camping, unattended micro-sites, or purely self-service models, but most retained quantity still needs an operator.
o.change_evidence: Operating records from remote-managed and conventionally staffed parks comparing unattended check-in share, service incidents, regulatory staffing requirements, maintenance response, insurance claims, guest ratings, and full-time-equivalent staffing per occupied site-night would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The estimate concerns the need for an accountable operator, not the amount of on-site labor.
Local health, fire, pool, lodging, and utility rules vary and may raise the irreducible operator requirement.
The occupation mix includes recreational camps as well as RV parks and campgrounds.

narrative.executive_view: RV parks and campgrounds combine a meaningful administrative automation pocket with an operation that remains anchored in physical property care. The clearest opportunities are reservations, guest communications, bookkeeping, marketing, scheduling, occupancy optimization, and pricing; the larger bodies of maintenance, grounds, housekeeping, safety, and recreation work are much less substitutable. Private operating firms generally fit the external-customer lens, but land-heavy structures and family ownership complicate transferability. Demand looks durable in the mixed camping basket, yet the 2025 decline in RV participation argues against assuming growth.
narrative.ai_impact: AI and established campground systems can answer routine questions, take and modify bookings, process payments, send pre-arrival and in-stay messages, draft marketing, support bookkeeping, forecast occupancy, recommend prices, and optimize the site grid. Human work remains important for exceptions, complaints, sales judgment, safety, maintenance triage, guest recovery, and supervision. Physical execution across utilities, grounds, washrooms, housekeeping, repairs, food, and activities remains the limiting boundary.
narrative.value_capture: Because accommodation and add-ons are sold at market prices rather than billed as reimbursed labor, savings can initially accrue to the operator. Scarce, well-located parks with constrained new supply may retain more through margin and reinvestment. Frequent repricing, online comparison, public alternatives, and guest expectations for visible service and amenities will share part of the benefit through price competition or service investment over five years.
narrative.firm_availability: The industry definition largely describes active guest-facing operators, but the underlying asset often combines land, infrastructure, owner residence, and a family business. Sector brokers report active mid-market and value-add buying, while campground succession guidance emphasizes family transfers and emotional and estate complexity. A qualifying sale is plausible but far from automatic, and the key diligence need is a denominator-based record of operating-company control transfers rather than property listings.
narrative.demand_durability: Camping participation remains above pre-pandemic benchmarks and vehicle-accessible camping grew in 2025, supporting continued need for sites and facilities. However, KOA reported a slight year-over-year reset in 2024 and OIA reported a 6.4% RV-camping decline in 2025. Fuel and RV costs, weather, public-site competition, and discretionary travel budgets create downside; affordability, domestic travel, constrained zoning, and broad tent and vehicle participation support the base case.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central evidence gaps are exact-721211 occupation and automation data, representative independent-park software adoption and realized labor savings, a firm-level eligibility census, and a completed control-transfer denominator. Vendor revenue claims may overstate typical results, and participation surveys do not isolate paid private site-nights. Seasonality, local regulation, catastrophe exposure, utility infrastructure, and the inseparability of operations from real estate can dominate the economics even when administrative automation works.
narrative.principal_driver: A concentrated office, reservation, customer-service, pricing, and management workflow layer can be standardized across otherwise fragmented physical park operations.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most labor and nearly all service continuity remain tied to site-specific physical operations, while land-heavy family ownership makes eligible control transfers uncertain.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 721200
sources.supports: Direct BLS occupation mix for the broader RV parks and recreational camps industry: 64,480 workers; management 12.00%, building and grounds 16.08%, personal care and service 13.78%, office and administrative support 19.34%, and installation, maintenance and repair 16.14%, with detailed desk clerk, customer service, reservation, bookkeeping, grounds, housekeeping, recreation, and repair occupations.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An early look at the labor market impact potential of large language models
sources.supports: Broad occupational task-exposure context: about 80% of the U.S. workforce could have at least 10% of tasks affected and about 19% could have at least 50% affected; used only as context, not as a direct campground estimate.

sources.title: Large Firms With At Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Census BTOS evidence that U.S. business AI use was 17% to 20% from December 2025 to May 2026, expected use was 20% to 23%, and fewer than 20% of firms with four or fewer employees reported use.

sources.title: Increase Campground Revenue
sources.supports: Direct evidence that campground software automates dynamic pricing, site-grid optimization, reservation add-ons, and marketplace booking; Campspot states more than 3,500 U.S. and Canadian campgrounds use it and reports specific 2025 network revenue effects, subject to vendor-selection caveats.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Learning curves
sources.supports: Observed Claude usage across occupations and workflows: 49% of jobs had at least one-quarter of tasks represented in Claude use, and customer-service tasks such as payment and billing support showed high exposure in automated API workflows.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System, United States, 2022
sources.supports: Official definition of NAICS 721211 as operating sites for campers and equipment with facilities including washrooms, laundry, recreation halls, playgrounds, stores, and snack bars; excludes facilities without accommodations and residential mobile-home-site lessors.

sources.title: RV & MHP Investment Report 2025
sources.supports: Broker evidence of fragmented private ownership, a 2024 shift toward $1 million to $10 million mid-market deals, active private-equity and mid-market buyers, value-add acquisitions, seller financing, and zoning and capital barriers to new supply.

sources.title: WACO News July 2025: Preparing Your Campground to Carry On
sources.supports: Industry succession guidance describing owner residence, generational family ownership, emotional ties, blurred personal and business property, and campground value concentrated in land rather than ready cash.

sources.title: 2025 Camping & Outdoor Hospitality Report press release
sources.supports: KOA survey evidence: over 11 million more households camped in 2024 than in 2019, camping was slightly down year over year, 65% planned the same or more travel spending, and the study used 2,912 U.S. plus 1,207 Canadian household responses.

sources.title: 2026 Camping Report: Executive Summary
sources.supports: Outdoor Industry Association evidence that 42.3 million Americans camped near vehicles in 2025, up more than 5% in one year and 6.2 million since 2020, while RV camping fell 6.4% year over year and returned to pre-pandemic levels.
